reasoning: The informal claim is: for every integer n, if n is a sum of two rational squares then it is a sum of two integer squares. The Lean statement universally quantifies r, s : ℚ and n : ℤ and assumes r² + s² = n, concluding ∃ a, b : ℤ, a² + b² = n. By the standard equivalence (∃ x, P x) → Q ≡ ∀ x, P x → Q (when Q is independent of x), the "∀ r, s, r² + s² = n → ..." form is logically equivalent to "(∃ r, s, r² + s² = n) → ...". The types match: r² + s² = n uses the ℤ→ℚ coercion on n, and a² + b² = n is in ℤ. No extra or missing hypotheses, so the formalization is faithful.
